speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about the kibbutz preparation and the trade you were learning. You were learning to be a machinist. And what happened from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, this camp was closed out in 1939 or '41. And we were transported past Berlin, what was called [GERMAN], if that means anything. That was one. iT was a ranch , a big ranch where we worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other one was [GERMAN], also a ranch. We harvested potatoes and-- and-- and sweet bees-- sweet-- no. What did they call this? Whatever. It was work for maybe two, three weeks. And I after that was over, we ended up in Paderborn, Westphalia. It was a bigger camp. It was located on [GERMAN], if that means to some people. Friends of ours, friends of mine, they'll probably know. There, we worked for the city and did anything or everything what the city needed, from cleaning the streets, dumping the trash, working in unloading boxcars, even working in a-- in a-- in -- a-- they made marmalade. How do they say? They made jellies and-- Marmalades. --Marmalades.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the biggest one, and the best one, because there was a lot of sugar around. And on the way home, you stuck your pants full of sugar, trying to bring it home, have some to eat. And also, I can remember I worked in a place where the-- where dirty water from the-- from the city sewer came in. And it was a sewer treatment plant. And there I worked. Naturally, the smell was-- but they give you boots, rubber boots. And you shoveled dirt. You know, the water-- they separate the water from the dirt, what came out of the sewers, and so on. It was a dirty, dirty thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you treated in these camps? Were these work camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, I was-- we were treated nice. There was no-- no real, you know-- nobody kicked you or told you, hey, hurry up, and things like that. But you know, the people-- we were in civilian clothes. The only thing, you had the star. You had the star. You have to keep your star on, with Juden-- Jew-- written on it. And sometimes, in the wintertime, people, ordinary people, they see you, and first they looked around left and right, and nobody sees them, they might have given you an apple or give you something to eat. So you know, they were sympathetic to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you there in those camps simply because there was sort of a lack of alternative at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, where-- I don't know. There was nothing else. There was nobody else left in Germany. And we, as the young people, were the only ones. Now, there's a story I didn't tell before, but I would like to really put it in. My father and mother were still in Cologne.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had traveled later and followed you to Cologne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they followed me to Cologne. And I, you know, I seen them once in every two weeks from the kibbutz [HEBREW] and went to them and, you know, and had maybe dinner and things like that. But in Paderborn, in 1942, all of a sudden, the head of the-- the organization there, the kibbutz-- what was his name, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Stein? Or what-- it doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was called to the telephone. Manfred, your father is on the telephone. My father? Yes, come in here. So we went in the office. And he and my father called. He said, Manfred, we will be evacuated within the next four or five days. Do you want to come with us? We want you should come with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, Papa, no. [SPEAKING GERMAN] I don't want to come. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew already what it all was, naturally. And my mother then got to the phone. And then she said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- he's crying. So I use some kind of a-- maybe it was a stupid idea-- a stupid excuse, that I said, Papa, I think I can stay here, and maybe I can help you from here, wherever you go. And with that, the conversation ended. That was the last time I talked to them. And I knew where they were going, and I knew from other parents who called their children to come home. Some of them did go. And I don't know. I just had a funny feeling not to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1942. Well, I was 14. '24--

speaker: interviewee
text: 18. Not 14-- 18. So once they left, they were gone. All of a sudden, the same command came to us to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get-- get everything together. Get a rucksack. Put in two-- two socks, two shirts, two this, two that. OK. And get ready to-- at least to have enough to wear for a week. They didn't say anything about food or anything, just to wear. And as I mentioned once, too, the head of the city in Paderborn was also an SS officer. He always said, [GERMAN]. I command-- I'm the head of mein Juden. I'm the head of the Jews here, and they don't go. But actually, he could not overrule the command from Berlin. So-- so we went. We had to go. From Paderborn, we went to the next town. First, we went from Paderborn in a regular coach to Bielefeld. Bielefeld was another town. From there we got pushed into boxcars. And things get already worse in that way. So we-- we--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think? Your being put into--

speaker: interviewee
text: What did we think? Well, what was it? I don't know. We-- I-- we didn't know what to think. Just that it didn't look-- it didn't look good. We all were crammed in the boxcars, and there was one pail, if you had to do your thing, what you had to do. And waiting for-- a way-- the boxcar was still, you could open it and throw things out. I can remember, I took my-- my-- all my belongings and pushed them in a corner next to a window. So I had visual-- actually visual-- I could see where we were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: We came by-- I think it was Dresden that time. Dresden was bombed real bad in 1943. And you could see the flames from the train. The people who worked on the train came by, and they point it out, you see? Can you see? The Americans, the British-- they are the ones who did it. For me, it was just a happy feeling, actually. But I didn't know myself, where do I end up, you know? Well, we traveled at least a week or so, or was it four or five days. You know, time-- you didn't have a calendar [LAUGHS] to tell you what day it is and where are we. Well-- Were you given food or water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food-- I don't remember what was with that food. They get-- they gave you some food. They gave you some water. I think they opened up the-- the-- the boxcars, and I think we got some food. It wasn't very much. My recollection isn't that-- that good about that. I always felt, OK. It's enough in here. You're more scared than anything else. So finally, we-- we got to what was Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What time of the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was in the night. It was in the middle of the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe exactly what you saw and how you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I can do that. It was first we stopped, and I think we were laid off. They waited until nightfall. That's what they always did, I was told. At nightfall, finally, the train moved, and we got into Auschwitz itself. The boxcars opened up, and screams and holler. Rouse! Rouse! Rouse! Hurry up! Fast! Make sure! Get out! Get out! All what you-- are in there-- [GERMAN] leave it alone! Don't touch! That will come after. First, you get out. We'll bring you the other things later. Don't worry about it. The only thing-- you had your clothes, your shoes, or whatever you had on. And loudspeakers started calling, anybody who's sick, women, children, on the right-hand side, over there. And anybody who feels he's sick, same place. Men who are over 45, also over there. Others from 18 up, they can stay right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stood there. I stood with my fellow men and friends. And here, I was a little guy, as you can see. And we come-- we stood in line. And I was-- I stood in the front line.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here comes by an SS officer. He could have been Untersturmfuhrer. He could have been Obersturmfuhrer. I mean, I was not that much that I knew what rank he really had. He came by, and he had good boots on with a-- with a-- with a whip from horse, a horse whip.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he hit that horse whip, always, on his boots. And he looked, you know, with a stern look, from one place and one guy to the other. All of a sudden, he stopped right in front of me. [GERMAN]? I'm 18. Mm-hm. And I stood in attention. I clicked my heels, according to the German military way. And [GERMAN]? Do you know how to work? I said, yes, I do. OK. You can stay now. So that was the first break, I would say. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, we got loaded up in military trucks, no tarp on. The side on-- on the trucks was about that high. And we had to hold on, one to each other, so we wouldn't fall out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the sky was clear. I see the stars. And I said to myself, God, what did I do? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just a young man. I'm only 18. I'm no criminal. That was my thoughts, OK? And I told him, God, please, just register that that's how I feel.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was that episode. And he came-- then we came into-- it was a-- was a-- what they called [GERMAN], another smaller camp, what was called [GERMAN]. And we came in there, out of the trucks, into a-- into a wash room with showers. And they had showers up on top. And everybody-- everything out. [GERMAN]. Except you keep your boots. Everything else, you just put on the side. I had a watch. Grandma used to give me that watch for bar mitzvah. And it was an eight-corner watch. And I felt, they're not going to get that watch. And there were-- there were wash basins. And I took the watch, and I smashed it in there, so nobody could ever use it. And then they-- they said, OK, under the shower. There is a piece of soap. The soap has to last for five, six people. First of all, we make you wet. Well, when the water came down, it was cold. [LAUGHS] And then finally, it got warm, a little warm, and then they screamed, hurry up! Soap yourself in, fast! No fooling around. And so you soaped yourself in as fast as you could, and then they washed you off, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came out, naked, as you were. They-- after that, they have you standing outside. It was cold. And from there, they moved you in a barrack. And then they're starting clipping your hair. Wherever there was a little hair, they took it-- wherever-- and-- till you felt like a chicken with plucked feathers, you know? Like a chicken looks like there is nothing on it but just the stubble of the-- of the-- of the-- of the, you know, from your-- from your-- from your skin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they-- all of a sudden, they come. Here's the clothes. It was already the striped pajamas. And they were wet. They came out of a cleaners probably. They didn't warm-- they didn't clean them. I mean, they cleaned them, but they did not dry them. So, put it on. Put it on. That's-- they count it off that-- block number 14, block number 7. So many go in here. So many go in there. And that's it. So we headed on. And here, we were in the-- in the-- in the barracks. The beds were three of them. I think the first night, they told us, don't you lay down. You leave every bed alone. Look at it and make sure that you learn how to make it tomorrow. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then we got out. Next morning-- [COUGHS] excuse me-- get daylight. Then they had us all lined up again alphabetically. Then they started tattooing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Peter, a friend of mine-- we were together so long. His name is also Wolf-- He stood right after me. And my number was 105064, and his was 65. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show us your tattoo again? Maybe horizontally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you see it? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you read the number for us again?

speaker: interviewee
text: 105064. 105,064. We were the 105,000, 104,000, up to, I think, 107. They were all German Jews in that transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your friend Peter was standing right next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he has the following number.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He has the next number.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about this tattoo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew-- well, what I forgot to tell, when we came in to the wash room, here comes a fellow who's been in there already [? since ?] years. He was-- he was-- he was German, but the parents came from Poland, and they were not really born citizens of Germany. He's been there already. He was in Buchenwald. He was in Sachsenhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name is Freddie. Freddie Nyman. He lives here in the Valley. And he looked at us, and he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? You know you are here. You know you'll never get out. That's the end of it. Don't you know? How come-- how come you get yourself caught to bring you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never forget that, too. Freddie knows it. I told him many times. So that was, you know-- that was the welcome, so to speak of. Then, after that, you know, you knew where you are. And it was-- that was concentration camp. And you probably connect-- we didn't know anything about the gas chamber, really. We learned about that later.

speaker: interviewer
text: That, you know, you-- if you're-- [INAUDIBLE] here, you go through the chimney. I said, what do you mean? [GERMAN]. That means the crematorium was built next to the gas chamber. And after people were dead, they hauled them out with-- with-- with little wagons and burned them. Went through the chimney-- that's what it meant. In the camp where you were, what kind of work did they have you do? What was your daily routine there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had-- one thing-- I was lucky, when I came in, and I ended up on block vierzehn-- Barrack number 14. The head of the barracks had a-- was a political. Had a red triangle. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: That meant a political prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm? Yeah. It meant a political prisoner. Somehow, we got in a conversation, and I told him-- he said he's from [GERMAN] Oh. I said, yeah, my [SPEAKING GERMAN] come from [GERMAN] from [GERMAN]. Oh yeah. [GERMAN], really? Yes. So he's like, hey, come on. I'll take care of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- he kept me that I and everybody else in the morning got out. We had to clean. But that meant two or three extra bowls of soup or food. So that was a great help. And he-- it went on for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything has a beginning. Everything has an end. So it-- it stopped somehow. And what-- another thing, what was very important when I-- what happened to me, one day, in the beginning, in the first maybe three, four weeks, everybody marched out to work. And somebody comes over and said, you-- you don't have to go. You can stay here. You take it easy. Just stay here. I said, yeah, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So next to block vierzehn was block zwolf-- number 12. They said, OK, you go in here. I go in there. And two guys were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was a little fellow. He was the [GERMAN], they called him. That means, first comes the block [GERMAN]-- that means the head of the barracks. The [GERMAN] was the man who took care of that everything looks clean, everything is fine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then then he told me, put your pants down. I said, what do you mean, put your pants down? Put your pants down. Bend over. Bend over. And here, they rape me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I mean, I was stunned. I didn't even know what happened to me. What are they doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then they tell me, OK. You shut your mouth. If you say anything, we'll tell the SS that you wanted this all. So after the day was over, then I went back to the old barracks, and I was still-- you know, in-- first thing that ever happened to me. And-- but then later, every day you had to line up on the appellplatz-- what is called, in English? It's the-- where do the soldiers go to count them, and everybody has to-- The roll call.

speaker: interviewee
text: A roll call, right-- make sure that they're all there. We had to stand five deep, each one. And then they-- then they, you know, roll call, they ask how many in your block? How many-- how many in your command are out here? And then the SS, who stood outside, they added it up, naturally. OK, everybody's there. Fine. Ready. We march out into the factory. And you-- what I told you-- this-- this IG Farben-- it's something, like, similar to DuPont. And you had to march out. They had a-- they had a-- there was a music stand also, on the right-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you marched out?

speaker: interviewee
text: As we marched out. Was an [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] No. How would they say this in English? A--

speaker: interviewer
text: Chapel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. No, not a chapel. What do you call a-- people together making music with so many instruments?

speaker: interviewer
text: A band.

speaker: interviewee
text: A band-- there you go. Thank you. [LAUGHS] A band. The German and English, when you do this, it's hard to look for the rights words. It was a band.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they playing as you marched out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They were playing, naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: What music did they play? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any-- I don't recall right away, OK. They played music, and you had to go by. And then one of the SS used to say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That means you take your hat off. It's like saluting, so to speak of. And then you kept on walking. And once you pass the stand, the viewing stand, with the band, then [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Then you kept on walking into the factory, into the place where we were. And many times, on the way down there, there was-- there was guards on either side, naturally. And if they wanted to kill someone, they took the cap away from this person, from the prisoner, and threw it in the field. And then the guard said, go! Get it! That was death. That's all. They wanted you to get out of the-- out of the-- the-- the-- the surrounding of the-- of the-- of the guards. Then you were like you want to run away. They had a reason. Then they killed them. And then they screamed, down! Everybody on the floor. And the poor guy was running, and they shot him. Happened many times. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then in the IG Farben there, in this big-- Factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Factory. They gave me a job drilling holes in the wire boxes-- boxes, electrical, the wire boxes. They had four or five openings where they put steel pipes in and then ran the wires, the electrical wire, through, in order to-- to, you know, get the electrical thing going, with lights and everything. Then, once I had-- there was a lot of Polish people there. They were not so kosher, I would say. Poles, I'm sorry, but they were always a little bit anti-Semitic. There was a fellow, a young guy, you know, who always looked at us, or looked at me. I said, I didn't know why. Why the hell he always looked at me. I had boots. My father bought me those boots. They were up to here. Just like the SS wore.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to stop right here. This is the end of tape three, interview with Fred Wolf.
